prompt:
Feature-flag lookups hit Redis on every request and the ops bill is silly; we want a tiny in-process TTL cache in front of it. My guess was that a sync.Map with a janitor goroutine would do, but the platform folks vetoed background goroutines in this library, so: lazy expiry on read plus an explicit sweep the caller can schedule.

Package ttlmap at the root of module go-ttl-map, stdlib only:
- type Clock interface { Now() time.Time } — injected; New(clock Clock) (*Map, error), nil clock is an error
- Set(key string, value any, ttl time.Duration) — entry expires at Now()+ttl. The deadline itself counts as expired (a Get exactly at Now()+ttl is a miss). ttl <= 0 means the entry never expires. Overwriting a key replaces the value AND restarts the TTL from the overwrite.
- Get(key string) (any, bool) — a hit returns the value; a miss returns (nil, false). Reading an expired entry must DELETE it right then (read-expiry), not just skip it — otherwise Sweep double-counts, which is exactly the bug class I want tested away.
- Sweep() int — remove every expired entry, return how many were removed. Entries already removed by read-expiry must not be counted again.
- Len() int and Keys() []string (sorted) — both must report LIVE entries only, even if expired corpses haven't been swept yet.
- Delete(key string) bool — true only if the key was present AND still live.

All of it concurrent-safe; request goroutines call Get/Set while a cron calls Sweep. ttlmap_test.go is the contract (protected file, don't touch). Definition of done: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... green.
